title: Detecting arthropod leaf and flower visitation using DNA metabarcoding
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"2magt","text":"This protocol is designed for extracting DNA from plant material or swabs for metabarcoding to detect and identify ecosystem service and disservice providers and their interactions with plants. The reagents and methods proposed offer a cost effective and high-throughput method for molecular analysis using standard lab equipment. Where specialist equipment is used, attempts are made to suggest low-cost alternatives.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"d1omr","text":"Check safety guidelines for individual reagents before commencing work. Some reagents will be toxic, corrosive or otherwise present health and safety risks. Appropriate personal protective equipment should be used at all times, not only for personal safety but also reduction of contamination risk.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Select suitable sites and locations for plant surveying. This protocol is based on hand-collected leaves and flowers, or swabs of leaves and flowers, but other collection methods may be viable; be wary of potential sources of contamination though. Consider how systematic the study needs to be and the various constraints imposed on the data by the study design.
Add 200 μL of the supernatant from STEP 10 into each well containing Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol.

Per 96-well plate, the Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol master mix should comprise: A B C 1 Reagent Amount per sample Amount per 96-well plate 2 Protein Denaturation Buffer 220 μL 42.2 mL 3 Ethanol (100 %) 220 μL 42.2 μL

Add all of the sample solution (~ 600 μL) to a well in a 96-well DNA membrane spin column and cover with a breathable seal. Ensure there is a suitable reservoir beneath into which the flow-through will go (e.g., 2.2 mL deep-well plate).
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 10 minutes) will work.
Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 1 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work.

Wash Buffer 1 should be comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Guanidine HCl 7 M 95.53 29.4 g 3 Ethanol 56 % - 56 mL 4 H2O - - 44 mL
Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 2 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

Wash Buffer 2 should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Ethanol (100 %) 70 % 58.44 70 mL 3 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 4 H2O - - 29 mL
Centrifuge at 20,000 x g for 3 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 20,000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 15 minutes) will work.

Carefully move the spin column plate to a new 0.5 mL DNA collection plate. The liquid level following the final wash will be close to the base of the spin column, so take care not to let it touch to prevent ethanol transfer to the soon-to-be eluted DNA.
Add 200 μL Elution Buffer directly to the silica membrane and leave it at room temperature for 5 minutes, covering with a breathable seal.

Elution Buffer should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 3 H2O - - 99 mL

Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute. The DNA is now in the collection plate and can be taken forward to amplification. Steps 20-21 can be repeated for increased DNA yield but a lower overall concentration. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work, although should not be necessary for this step.
Add 400 μL of master mix of Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol (detailed below) to each well of the 2.2 ml 96-well plate.

Protein Denaturation Buffer should be comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Guanidine HCl 5 M 95.53 47.7 g 3 H2O - - 100 mL

Per 96-well plate, the Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol master mix should comprise: A B C 1 Reagent Amount per sample Amount per 96-well plate 2 Protein Denaturation Buffer 220 μL 21.1 mL 3 Ethanol (100 %) 220 μL 21.1 μL

Add all of the sample solution (~ 600 μL) to a well in a 96-well silica membrane spin column and cover with a breathable seal. Ensure there is a suitable reservoir beneath into which the flow-through will go (e.g., 2.2 mL deep-well plate).
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 10 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 10 minutes) will work.
Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 1 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

Wash Buffer 1 should be comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Guanidine HCl 7 M 95.53 29.4 g 3 Ethanol 56 % - 56 mL 4 H2O - - 44 mL
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 5 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work.
Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 2 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

Carefully move the spin column plate to a new 0.5 mL DNA collection plate. The liquid level following the final wash will be close to the base of the spin column, so take care not to let it touch to prevent ethanol transfer to the soon-to-be eluted DNA.

Wash Buffer 2 should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Ethanol (100 %) 70 % 58.44 70 mL 3 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 4 H2O - - 29 mL
Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 15 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 20,000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 15 minutes) will work.
Add 200 μL Elution Buffer directly to the silica membrane and leave it at room temperature for 5 minutes, covering with a breathable seal.

Elution Buffer should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 3 H2O - - 99 mL

Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 2 minutes. The DNA is now in the collection plate and can be taken forward to amplification. Steps 20-21 can be repeated for increased DNA yield but a lower overall concentration. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work, although should not be necessary for this step.
Grind the larvae in a Geno/Grinder 2010 at full speed (1750 RPM) for 4 minutes. If the larvae are not fully homogenised, repeat this step.
Centrifuge at 4,000 g for 2 minutes.
Remove the ball bearings using a magnet, taking care not to cross-contaminate the samples.
To the lysate, add 1 mL of freshly-prepared Proteinase Buffer master mix of Lysis Buffer 2 and proteinase K (detailed in the sub-steps below) and vortex to mix.

Lysis Buffer 2 should be pH 9 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Sodium chloride 120 mM 58.44 0.701 g 3 1 M Tris-Hcl 50 mM - 5 mL 4 0.5 M EDTA 20 mM - 4 mL 5 SDS 3 % - 3 g 6 H2O - - 91 mL
Per sample, the Proteinase Buffer master mix should comprise: A B 1 Reagent Amount per plate 2 Lysis solution 2 102.4 mL 3 Proteinase K (10 mg/mL) 3.168 mL
Incubate at 37 °C overnight (12-16 hours) All proteins including Thaumetopoeitin are now inactivated.
Centrifuge at 4000 x g for 4 minutes.
Transfer 1 mL of the supernatant to a clean 2.2 mL deep-well 96-well plate for archiving/backup and retain the rest in the original plate.
Centrifuge at 2000 x g for 4 minutes.
Transfer 200 μL of the supernatant to a clean 2.2 mL deep-well 96-well plate. The remaining lysate can now be stored at -20 °C for backup/future work.

Add two 3 mm hardened carbon steel beads to each 5 mL sample tube. Beads are usually shipped coated in manufacturing oil (especially the carbon steel beads). To remove this, place beads in a borosilicate glass beaker or Duran bottle with plastic pouring lip and lid removed then bake for at least 12 hours at 250 °C.

Following collection, store samples in sterile tubes (e.g., 5 mL centrifuge tubes). For flowers and leaves, store samples dry; for swabs, storage should similarly be dry except for the TNES used for swabbing. Only take as much material as is necessary to minimise any impact.

Transfer individual swabs into deep-well (e.g., 2.2 mL) 96-well plates. Consider the distribution of experimental controls ahead of subsequent steps to streamline downstream liquid handling. Plant material will transfer to a plate format later, but this is still important to consider early on; instead, plant material can be placed into separate 5 mL centrifuge tubes. Our recommended PCR plate layout, which could be adopted here for streamlining downstream. Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/559f6hh Store samples at -20 °C until ready to process.
Centrifuge the tubes at 2000 x g, Room temperature, 2 minutes to separate solid material from the supernatant.

Two protocols are presented below, which simply differ in whether samples are ground or not. For plant material, we recommend grinding to facilitate analysis of internal DNA (e.g., internal herbivores like leaf miners). For swabs, we recommend simply soaking to extract DNA (otherwise exactly the same protocol, but different initial treatment). These protocols could both be used for a wider range of potential applications.

Grind the samples in a tissue grinder/homogeniser/lyser at 1750 rpm, Room temperature, 30 seconds . You can repeat this step until the sample is adequately homogenised, but so long as most of the tissue is homogenised, that should suffice.

Prepare Kingfisher Apex reagent plates as below. The Kingfisher Apex is ideal for automated high-throughput extraction of DNA, but will not always be available. Alternative equipment can achieve similar results, including just using magnetic racks. In that case, rather than transferring beads between plates, remove the supernatant and replace it with the next reagent, as described for normalisation below.

Add 10 µL 20 mg/mL papain to each well. For streamlining, you could add the papain prior to grinding, but be aware that this may reduce its efficacy. If using larger volumes of TNES, adjust papain volumes accordingly.
Incubate overnight (~ 16:00:00 ) at 37 °C .
Insert plates into the Kingfisher Apex and run a preset programme with the below steps.

Using tagged primers, unique identifiers can be established for a full plate with 8 uniquely tagged forward primers and 12 uniquely tagged reverse primers. For ease, if able to use a 96-well pipette, consider creating a "primer plate" containing both PCR primers for each well at 5 µM concentration; this is especially effective when using multiple plates. For 25 µL reaction volumes, this will subsequently involve adding 22.75 µL hot-start Taq polymerase and water master mix (described in the step above) to each well, followed by 1.25 µL of each primer mix to its corresponding well. It is possible to do this with a multichannel pipette. Distribution of tagged PCR primers across the 96-well plate. Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/559f6hh
Add 1 µL DNA to each corresponding sample or positive control well, and 1 µL molecular grade water to each negative control other than extraction negative control(s).
Briefly centrifuge the plate to ensure that the oil is above the PCR mix and everything is at the bottom of each well without air bubbles.

Load the PCR plate into a thermocycler. Ensure that the temperature regime matches the enzyme used (including any heat activation for hot-start Taq) and that the annealing temperature matches the PCR primers used. Given differences between labs and samples, and inaccuracies in temperature calibration, considering running a temperature gradient PCR with known samples to check the specificity of your PCR primers.

For the sample plate, to each well of a 96-well deep-well plate, add 60 µL of the filtered lysate from STEP 14, 120 µL of 1.5X GITC buffer, 120 µL of 1 mg/mL SeraMag Speed Beads in TE and 240 µL isopropanol. For the 1.5X GITC buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 6 Molarity (M) GITC, 75 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 3 % volume sarkosyl, 30 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 0.15 % volume antifoam).
For the two ethanol plates, to each well of two 96-well deep-well plates, add 400 µL 80 % ethanol.
For the isopropanol plate, to each well of a 96-well deep-well plate, add 400 µL isopropanol.
For the elution plate, to each well of a 96-well standard plate, add 100 µL molecular biology grade water.
Pick up the 96 deep-well tip comb from a 96-well standard plate.
Bind DNA to the beads by mixing at medium speed for 00:05:00 with a slow post-mix for 00:05:00 .
Collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Release the beads into the isopropanol plate and mix for 00:01:00 at medium speed, and collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Release the beads into one of the 80 % ethanol plates and mix for 00:01:00 at medium speed, and collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Release the beads into one of the 80 % ethanol plates and mix for 00:01:00 at medium speed, and collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Dry the beads above the well for 00:02:00 .
Release the beads into the elution plate and mix for 00:02:00 at fast speed with a slow post-mix for 00:03:00 , and collect the beads in four 00:00:20 collections.
Leave the tip comb in an empty 96-well standard plate.
Store the eluted DNA at -20 °C until ready for subsequent steps.

Decide how samples will be distributed across plates (but don't distribute the DNA yet). Consider including a negative control in each row and column to detect any contaminants in each tagged forward and reverse primer. Among these wells, include any DNA extraction negative controls. Include positive controls (ideally mixed samples of species not found in the same study system), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). Include blank controls (ideally wells into which no reagents or at least no primers are added), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). If using multiple PCR primer pairs, familiarise yourself with the annealing temperatures for each and prepare separate PCR plates for each. For optimal accuracy, consider running replicates of each reaction (e.g., triplicates). Our recommended PCR plate layout, which could be adopted here for streamlining downstream. Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/559f6hh

Prepare enough PCR mastermix for each sample. For a full plate, the below values will usually suffice (with some overage to account for pipetting error), but check your specific Taq polymerase mix for any differences: %s %s These values are for 25 µL reaction volumes with 1 µL of template given the potentially high concentration of inhibitors in plant tissue samples, although smaller reaction volumes should be accurate too. Consider running them in triplicate for more accurate results.

Distribute one drop of mineral oil into each well of the PCR plate(s) (~ 20 µL ). This can be achieved by taking a large volume of mineral oil into the pipette tip and then gently depressing the plunger so that a drop forms and falls from the tip into each well.Mineral oil improves sealing of reactions by preventing evaporation and condensation. By reducing evaporation and thus loss of product, this also reduces potential cross-contamination.

The samples should now be checked for successful amplification, contamination in negatives and any secondary banding. Gel electrophoresis will achieve this, but digital systems like the Qiagen Qiaxcel will do this and facilitate equalisation by generating amplicon-specific DNA concentations.
Run your PCR programme.

The DNA extraction protocol is largely adapted from the BOMB-Bio tissue nucleic acid extraction protocol. See their documentation for additional detail. Bio-On-Magnetic-Beads (BOMB): Open platform for high-throughput nucleic acid extraction and manipulation..

Add 500 µL TNES lysis buffer to each well of the 96-well plate containing the swabs. For the TNES buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 100 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 52 millimolar (mM) NaCl, 10 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 10 Mass / % volume SDS).
Add 50 µL 20 mg/mL papain to each well. For streamlining, you could add the papain prior to grinding, but be aware that this may reduce its efficacy.
Incubate overnight (~ 16:00:00 ) at 37 °C .

Decide how samples will be distributed across plates (but don't distribute the DNA yet). Consider including a negative control in each row and column to detect any contaminants in each tagged forward and reverse primer. Among these wells, include any DNA extraction negative controls. Include positive controls (ideally mixed samples of species not found in the same study system), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). Include blank controls (ideally wells into which no reagents or at least no primers are added), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). If using multiple PCR primer pairs, familiarise yourself with the annealing temperatures for each and prepare separate PCR plates for each. For optimal accuracy, consider running replicates of each reaction (e.g., triplicates).

Prepare enough PCR mastermix for each sample. For a full plate, the below values will usually suffice (with some overage to account for pipetting error), but check your specific Taq polymerase mix for any differences: %s %s These values are for 25 µL reaction volumes, with 5 µL of template given the potentially low concentration of target DNA on swabs, although smaller reaction volumes should be accurate too. Consider running them in triplicate for more accurate results.

Using tagged primers, unique identifiers can be established for a full plate with 8 uniquely tagged forward primers and 12 uniquely tagged reverse primers. For ease, if able to use a 96-well pipette, consider creating a "primer plate" containing both PCR primers for each well at 5 µM concentration; this is especially effective when using multiple plates. For 25 µL reaction volumes, this will subsequently involve adding 18.75 µL hot-start Taq polymerase and water master mix (described in the step above) to each well, followed by 1.25 µL of each primer mix to its corresponding well. It is possible to do this with a multichannel pipette. Distribution of tagged PCR primers across the 96-well plate
Add 5 µL DNA to each corresponding sample or positive control well, and 5 µL molecular grade water to each negative control other than extraction negative control(s).

Distribute one drop of mineral oil into each well of the PCR plate(s) (~ 20 µL ). This can be achieved by taking a large volume of mineral oil into the pipette tip and then gently depressing the plunger so that a drop forms and falls from the tip into each well.Mineral oil improves sealing of reactions by preventing evaporation and condensation. By reducing evaporation and thus loss of product, this also reduces potential cross-contamination.
Briefly centrifuge the plate to ensure that the oil is above the PCR mix and everything is at the bottom of each well without air bubbles.

Load the PCR plate into a thermocycler. Ensure that the temperature regime matches the enzyme used (including any heat activation for hot-start Taq) and that the annealing temperature matches the PCR primers used. Given differences between labs and samples, and inaccuracies in temperature calibration, considering running a temperature gradient PCR with known samples to check the specificity of your PCR primers.
Run your PCR programme.

The samples should now be checked for successful amplification, contamination in negatives and any secondary banding. Gel electrophoresis will achieve this, but digital systems like the Qiagen Qiaxcel will do this and facilitate equalisation by generating amplicon-specific DNA concentations.

Equalisation is more effective than normalisation, but requires amplicon concentration data. Both can be time and labour intensive though, so can be skipped if time restricted for large-scale projects, although at the expense of data recovery and evenness. This protocol presents equalisation, but see the protocol from which this is forked for a step-by-step description of normalisation. If the PCRs were replicated (i.e., each sample run multiple times for each PCR primer pair used), these can be merged together into one plate at this point, or carried forward separately. Keeping the replicates separate increases the number of libraries to prepare and sequence later, but better facilitates identification of inconsistencies between samples that may arise from contamination or error. To merge triplicates, assuming use of 25 µL reaction volumes, pipette 22 µL from each well of two of the three plates into the corresponding well of the third. Briefly centrifuge the merged plate to move the oil to the top of the product again. To avoid pipetting oil from the oil-sealed PCR products, plunge the pipette to the first stop and fully insert the pipette tip into the bottom of the well, then release sharply. The PCR product will be taken up quickly, whereas the relatively viscous oil will be taken up slowly, thus being outcompeted by the PCR product.

To purify these pools ahead of library preparation, prepare a 1X solid phase reversible immobilisation (SPRI) bead solution and bring it to room temperature. The below steps detail how to make this solution, but it is also commercially available.

If using beads such as Sera-Mag Magnetic SpeedBeads (carboxylated, 1 µm, 3 EDAC/PA5), take 1 mL of well-mixed bead solution and wash the beads twice with TE+Tween buffer ( 10 millimolar (mM) Tris base, 1 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 0.05 % volume Tween 20, pH 8.0) by magnetising the beads, removing the supernatant, adding the TE+Tween, remagnetising the beads and removing the supernatant, and repeating the addition and removal of TE+Tween once more.
To the beads, add the following mix: %s %s
Add 20 mL of 50 % volume PEG to the tube to reach a 1X bead solution (alongside making the 0.1X solution, this will be useful later).
Add 5 mL of 1X bead solution to 45 mL of the following mix to make a 0.1X solution: %s %s

If ≥ 20 µL of PCR product is available for each pool, pipette 20 µL of 1X SPRI bead solution into each corresponding well of a 96-well plate or individual tubes. If less DNA is available, add a volume of SPRI bead solution equivalent to the full available volume of PCR product to the each corresponding well of the new 96-well plate or individual tubes. When working with magnetic bead solutions, ensure they are at room temperature and fully mixed, with no residue at the bottom of the container. This is a good opportunity for size selection as well, especially if the PCR product contains any secondary bands. If using SPRI beads, adjust the volume added to select different fragment sizes. Refer to manufacturer details for more information on size selection. Larger volumes can be used, so long as the corresponding volume of beads is used. For volumes greater than 100 µL , consider doubling the ethanol and water volumes in the remaining steps of this section.

Add 20 µL of PCR product (or whatever volume of beads was used in the last step) to each corresponding well of 1X bead solution, avoiding oil, and mix by vortexing ( 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute ). To avoid pipetting oil from the oil-sealed PCR products, plunge the pipette to the first stop and fully insert the pipette tip into the bottom of the well, then release sharply. The PCR product will be taken up quickly, whereas the relatively viscous oil will be taken up slowly, thus being outcompeted by the PCR product.
Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .

Remove all but 5 µL of the mixture from each well via pipette without disturbing the beads, which should be settled on the magnet. We recommend leaving 5 µL behind simply to avoid pipetting the beads themselves, but this can be avoided with care and experience.
Add 200 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.
Remove the ethanol and add a further 200 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.

Remove the ethanol as completely as possible by pipetting and allow the beads to air-dry until the aggregation of magnetic beads transitions from 'glossy' (shiny reflection of light) to 'matte' (dull dark brown mass), but not so long that it dries completely (i.e., begins to turn a rusty red and shows cracks).
Add 11 µL molecular grade water to each well, shake at 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute and incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .
Remove 10 µL of the supernatant and place it into a new tube. This will be the library used in any subsequent library preparation.

Quantify the concentration of the library from the previous step (e.g., using Qubit dsDNA assay) and take this forward for library preparation. For Oxford Nanopore Technologies sequencing, follow the manufacturer protocols (or adaptations of them on here). For Illumina sequencing, consider protocols such as the one this is forked from if your primers include a Nextera overhang; otherwise, consider ligation-based library preparation methods. Check with your sequencing provider how many fmol they will need in how many µL. This will be based on the sequencer, sequencing cartridge and any QC processes they follow.
Centrifuge the plate at 2000 x g, Room temperature, 2 minutes .

Prepare Kingfisher Apex reagent plates as below. The Kingfisher Apex is ideal for automated high-throughput extraction of DNA, but will not always be available. Alternative equipment can achieve similar results, including just using magnetic racks. In that case, rather than transferring beads between plates, remove the supernatant and replace it with the next reagent, as described for normalisation below.

For the sample plate, to each well of a 96-well deep-well plate, add 60 µL of the lysate from STEP 10, 120 µL of 1.5X GITC buffer, 120 µL of 1 mg/mL SeraMag Speed Beads in TE and 240 µL isopropanol. For the 1.5X GITC buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 6 Molarity (M) GITC, 75 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 3 % volume sarkosyl, 30 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 0.15 % volume antifoam).
For the two ethanol plates, to each well of two 96-well deep-well plates, add 400 µL 80 % ethanol.
For the isopropanol plate, to each well of a 96-well deep-well plate, add 400 µL isopropanol.
For the elution plate, to each well of a 96-well standard plate, add 100 µL molecular biology grade water.
Insert plates into the Kingfisher Apex and run a preset programme with the below steps.
Pick up the 96 deep-well tip comb from a 96-well standard plate.
Bind DNA to the beads by mixing at medium speed for 00:05:00 with a slow post-mix for 00:05:00 .
Collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Release the beads into the isopropanol plate and mix for 00:01:00 at medium speed, and collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Release the beads into one of the 80 % ethanol plates and mix for 00:01:00 at medium speed, and collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Release the beads into one of the 80 % ethanol plates and mix for 00:01:00 at medium speed, and collect the beads in three 00:00:01 collections.
Dry the beads above the well for 00:02:00 .
Release the beads into the elution plate and mix for 00:02:00 at fast speed with a slow post-mix for 00:03:00 , and collect the beads in four 00:00:20 collections.
Leave the tip comb in an empty 96-well standard plate.
Store the eluted DNA at -20 °C until ready for subsequent steps.

For plant material (e.g., leaves, flowers), tissue can be taken and placed in a labeled tube (5 mL centrifuge tubes are recommended downstream) until it can be frozen in a laboratory. One flower head and 1-2 small leaves should suffice, depending on size. For swabs, a cotton swab can be dipped into TNES buffer and then each side of the swab brushed against the plant tissue in a rolling motion 4-8 times, turning against the brushing motion to maximise contact. For the TNES buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 100 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 52 millimolar (mM) NaCl, 10 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 10 Mass / % volume SDS).

Place each sample tube into an aluminium tube rack in a robust water-right container (e.g.,polystyrene delivery box). Pour liquid nitrogen over the sample and the block, allowing it to stand in liquid nitrogen for ~ 00:10:00 to cool sufficiently. This step is much quicker if the aluminium block is already pre-cooled to -20 °C prior to the extractions.

Add 100 µL of TNES to each sample. For larger samples, consider adding more (up to 500 µL to ensure coverage and mitigate subsequent collection of solid material). For the TNES buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 100 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 52 millimolar (mM) NaCl, 10 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 10 Mass / % volume SDS).

Transfer 100 µL to a 96-well filter plate with a 96-well plate positioned below to catch and retain the flow-through. Centrifuge at 2000 x g, °C, 2 minutes to separate the supernatant from the physical material and discard the filter plate. This is not referring to silica membrane spin columns, which are commonly used in spin column-based DNA extractions. Instead, these plates filter out physical material and let liquid through. If more than 100 µL TNES was added in STEP 10, you can transfer more to the filter plate, but take note of the filter and capture plate volumes and do not exceed them. If less than 100 µL can be collected, so long as 60 µL is available for STEP 15, that's okay. If that is not possible (e.g., due to physical plant material), add more TNES to dilute the lysate and take that forward.

Analyse the PCR products via digital electrophoresis (e.g., Qiagen Qiaxcel). This provides data on the concentration of specific amplicons (i.e., the target amplicon of the PCR primers used), which will be used in the next two steps. If digital electrophoresis (or an equivalent platform that facilitates quantification of concentration by band size) isn't available, you could proceed through the steps from STEP 27 in this section to remove primers and other non-target DNA, and quantify concentration using a fluorescence-based assay (e.g., Qubit dsDNA assay) to pool (as in STEPS 25-26) based on those values. It is important that primer dimers and other small fragments are removed (e.g., via magnetic bead-based purification) prior to these fluorescence-based assays as they can otherwise inflate and distort concentration values.

Based on the concentration of the target amplicon in each sample, calculate how much to pool from each sample in order to achieve equimolarity (i.e., equal representation of samples). This can be achieved by dividing the maximum concentration within the plate by the concentration of each individual sample.

Pool the value calculated for each sample in the previous step as a volume in microlitres. Positive controls can be treated as samples. For negative controls, pool the average volume pooled across the samples. For blanks, do not pool anything. If the volume to be pooled exceeds the total volume available, pool the total volume available. For samples with low or no concentration, the PCRs for these samples could be repeated, the samples could be excluded, or these can be pooled in the same manner as negative controls (although be wary in any interpretation of the data given increased potential for inaccuracy). If most of the volumes are similar and small, you can double (or otherwise multiply) the values. If there is a large variation in the volumes to be pooled, the values can be halved to facilitate pooling more dilute samples proportionally.

Equalisation is more effective than normalisation, but requires amplicon concentration data. Both can be time and labour intensive though, so can be skipped if time restricted for large-scale projects, although at the expense of data recovery and evenness. This protocol presents equalisation, but see the protocol from which this is forked for a step-by-step description of normalisation. If the PCRs were replicated (i.e., each sample run multiple times for each PCR primer pair used), these can be merged together into one plate at this point, or carried forward separately. Keeping the replicates separate increases the number of libraries to prepare and sequence later, but better facilitates identification of inconsistencies between samples that may arise from contamination or error. To merge triplicates, assuming use of 25 µL reaction volumes, pipette 22 µL from each well of two of the three plates into the corresponding well of the third. Briefly centrifuge the merged plate to move the oil to the top of the product again. To avoid pipetting oil from the oil-sealed PCR products, plunge the pipette to the first stop and fully insert the pipette tip into the bottom of the well, then release sharply. The PCR product will be taken up quickly, whereas the relatively viscous oil will be taken up slowly, thus being outcompeted by the PCR product.

Analyse the PCR products via digital electrophoresis (e.g., Qiagen Qiaxcel). This provides data on the concentration of specific amplicons (i.e., the target amplicon of the PCR primers used), which will be used in the next two steps. If digital electrophoresis (or an equivalent platform that facilitates quantification of concentration by band size) isn't available, you could proceed through the steps from STEP 31 in this section to remove primers and other non-target DNA, and quantify concentration using a fluorescence-based assay (e.g., Qubit dsDNA assay) to pool (as in STEPS 29-30) based on those values. It is important that primer dimers and other small fragments are removed (e.g., via magnetic bead-based purification) prior to these fluorescence-based assays as they can otherwise inflate and distort concentration values.

Based on the concentration of the target amplicon in each sample, calculate how much to pool from each sample in order to achieve equimolarity (i.e., equal representation of samples). This can be achieved by dividing the maximum concentration within the plate by the concentration of each individual sample.

Pool the value calculated for each sample in the previous step as a volume in microlitres. Positive controls can be treated as samples. For negative controls, pool the average volume pooled across the samples. For blanks, do not pool anything. If the volume to be pooled exceeds the total volume available, pool the total volume available. For samples with low or no concentration, the PCRs for these samples could be repeated, the samples could be excluded, or these can be pooled in the same manner as negative controls (although be wary in any interpretation of the data given increased potential for inaccuracy). If most of the volumes are similar and small, you can double (or otherwise multiply) the values. If there is a large variation in the volumes to be pooled, the values can be halved to facilitate pooling more dilute samples proportionally.

To purify these pools ahead of library preparation, prepare a 1X solid phase reversible immobilisation (SPRI) bead solution and bring it to room temperature. The below steps detail how to make this solution, but it is also commercially available.

If using beads such as Sera-Mag Magnetic SpeedBeads (carboxylated, 1 µm, 3 EDAC/PA5), take 1 mL of well-mixed bead solution and wash the beads twice with TE+Tween buffer ( 10 millimolar (mM) Tris base, 1 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 0.05 % volume Tween 20, pH 8.0) by magnetising the beads, removing the supernatant, adding the TE+Tween, remagnetising the beads and removing the supernatant, and repeating the addition and removal of TE+Tween once more.
To the beads, add the following mix: %s %s
Add 20 mL of 50 % volume PEG to the tube to reach a 1X bead solution (alongside making the 0.1X solution, this will be useful later).
Add 5 mL of 1X bead solution to 45 mL of the following mix to make a 0.1X solution: %s %s

If ≥ 20 µL of PCR product is available for each pool, pipette 20 µL of 1X SPRI bead solution into each corresponding well of a 96-well plate or individual tubes. If less DNA is available, add a volume of SPRI bead solution equivalent to the full available volume of PCR product to the each corresponding well of the new 96-well plate or individual tubes. When working with magnetic bead solutions, ensure they are at room temperature and fully mixed, with no residue at the bottom of the container. This is a good opportunity for size selection as well, especially if the PCR product contains any secondary bands. If using SPRI beads, adjust the volume added to select different fragment sizes. Refer to manufacturer details for more information on size selection. Larger volumes can be used, so long as the corresponding volume of beads is used. For volumes greater than 100 µL , consider doubling the ethanol and water volumes in the remaining steps of this section.

Add 20 µL of PCR product (or whatever volume of beads was used in the last step) to each corresponding well of 1X bead solution, avoiding oil, and mix by vortexing ( 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute ). To avoid pipetting oil from the oil-sealed PCR products, plunge the pipette to the first stop and fully insert the pipette tip into the bottom of the well, then release sharply. The PCR product will be taken up quickly, whereas the relatively viscous oil will be taken up slowly, thus being outcompeted by the PCR product.
Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .

Remove all but 5 µL of the mixture from each well via pipette without disturbing the beads, which should be settled on the magnet. We recommend leaving 5 µL behind simply to avoid pipetting the beads themselves, but this can be avoided with care and experience.
Add 200 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.
Remove the ethanol and add a further 200 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.

Remove the ethanol as completely as possible by pipetting and allow the beads to air-dry until the aggregation of magnetic beads transitions from 'glossy' (shiny reflection of light) to 'matte' (dull dark brown mass), but not so long that it dries completely (i.e., begins to turn a rusty red and shows cracks).
Add 11 µL molecular grade water to each well, shake at 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute and incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .
Remove 10 µL of the supernatant and place it into a new tube. This will be the library used in any subsequent library preparation.

Quantify the concentration of the library from the previous step (e.g., using Qubit dsDNA assay) and take this forward for library preparation. For Oxford Nanopore Technologies sequencing, follow the manufacturer protocols (or adaptations of them on here). For Illumina sequencing, consider protocols such as the one this is forked from if your primers include a Nextera overhang; otherwise, consider ligation-based library preparation methods. Check with your sequencing provider how many fmol they will need in how many µL. This will be based on the sequencer, sequencing cartridge and any QC processes they follow.
